scriptId: cs_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Medium-term plans for the next year
historyId: h_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Medium-term plans for the next year

memories.content:
- User asked for help turning ideas into a practical roadmap for the product team's medium-term plans for the next year.
- Assistant provided:
  - Quick clarifying questions to tailor the roadmap: product stage, team size and roles, top 1–3 strategic goals, hard constraints or deadlines, preferred roadmap format.
  - Recommended step-by-step process (8 steps): align vision & annual goals; collect & classify ideas; prioritize; define initiatives & milestones; build the timeline; assign resources & risks; communicate & get buy-in; review cadence.
  - Simple 12-month (quarterly) roadmap template with Q1–Q4, themes, initiatives, milestones, and key metrics.
  - Initiative one-page template (fields: title, problem statement, target outcome, success metrics, owner/team, timeline & milestones, effort/dependencies, risks & mitigation).
  - Prioritization example: RICE scoring formula and definitions (Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort).
  - Deliverables the assistant can produce now: draft quarterly roadmap from ideas, prioritized initiative list with RICE scoring,

The text argues for using both quarterly themes and overlapping timeboxed initiatives, outlining the benefits of each and providing a hybrid method. It states quarterly themes provide alignment, a single focus, easier communication, and prioritization, while overlapping timeboxed initiatives accommodate long-running work, staged milestones, and containment of experiments. It advises when to use each approach and recommends a hybrid method: set a single quarterly theme with 3–5 headline outcomes; categorize initiatives as quarter-owned, ongoing multi-quarter, and always-on; reserve quarterly capacity guardrails (e.g., 20–30% reliability, 10–20% experiments); break multi-quarter initiatives into quarterly milestones with explicit go/no-go checkpoints and measurable criteria; track dependencies and limit concurrent large epics across the same core team to 1–2; re-prioritize at the start of each

Stakeholders expect a polished demo of a flagship feature at a major industry event in September, making demo readiness a high-visibility deliverable. The reply states this is a high-priority fixed milestone to be treated as a hard constraint, recommending: lock a narrow demo scope; assign

- The user asked how much intentional slack to build into a one-year roadmap to absorb real customer feedback without derailing commitments.
- The response provides: 
  - Slack ranges: Capacity slack 15–30% of team capacity (with sub-ranges: 15% low risk/unknowns, 20% default, 25–30% high uncertainty/deadlines/staffing volatility); Budget contingency 10–15% of plan budget; Time buffers in schedule: 1 sprint per quarter and a 2–4 week stabilization window around major launches/demos.
